scoringRule: A 9 requires best-in-class, user-visible, main-path behavior with end-to-end or benchmark proof. Wired code, seeded fixtures, or narrative claims do not earn 9s.

9: Cursor/Copilot-feel FIM: low latency, high acceptance, stale-suggestion suppression, multi-line quality, and measured usage in real repos.
7: Main-path FIM is usable, tested, configurable, and has basic latency/quality telemetry, but trails frontier acceptance and polish.
5: Autocomplete exists but is inconsistent, weakly measured, or limited to happy-path fixtures.
3: Prototype-only completion path with little user-visible reliability.

9: Diagnostics, hover, symbols, definitions, and problem context are injected into live repair workflows with IDE smoke proof.
7: Diagnostics and problem-panel context are wired and tested, but semantic depth or workflow coverage is incomplete.
5: Some editor diagnostics are visible but not reliably used by the agent.
3: Diagnostics are present only as disconnected UI or tests.

9: Hybrid semantic search is fast, cited, large-repo capable, and proven to improve task completion.
7: Hybrid search is implemented and tested with relevance signals, but outcome lift or scale proof is thin.
5: Search exists but is mostly lexical or unmeasured.

9: Symbol graph, import graph, runtime traces, persistent cache, and citations reliably guide real code changes.
7: Repo map and dependency hints are integrated but lack runtime or large-scale proof.
5: Context is assembled from files with limited graph awareness.

9: Reproducible benchmark score is published with traces, pass/fail logs, and DanteForge on/off comparison.
7: Benchmark harness runs meaningful tranches but lacks public breadth or repeated validation.
5: Synthetic or narrow benchmark exists, with limited external comparability.
3: Harness scaffolding exists but no credible result.

9: Streaming edits, partial accept, hunk steering, rollback, and measured acceptance are polished in the editor.
7: Inline edits are user-visible and tested, but partial control or polish trails frontier tools.
5: Edits apply but interaction is coarse or brittle.
3: Edit path is mostly command-line or prototype-only.

9: Multi-file changes are risk-ranked, reviewable, reversible, and correlated with successful outcomes.
7: Diffs and review summaries are reliable, but severity ranking or false-positive handling is incomplete.
5: Multi-file edits work but are hard to review safely.

9: Branch, commit, PR, conflict, rollback, issue-to-PR, and review handoff work end-to-end.
7: Core git operations are strong, but PR/conflict automation or hosted handoff is incomplete.
5: Basic diffs and commits exist with gaps.
3: Git is mostly shell-driven by the user.

9: Vision-to-code creates responsive UI with visual-diff refinement and realistic acceptance tests.
7: Screenshot-to-code works for constrained UI with preview and tests.
5: Partial image-to-code path exists without robust visual refinement.

9: Generates, runs, inspects, repairs, tests, and deploys realistic apps across stacks.
7: Can generate useful apps with verification, but deployment or multi-stack proof is limited.
5: Scaffolds apps but often leaves manual completion.

9: Transcript, context controls, proof surfacing, commands, images, loading/error states, and recovery feel premium.
7: Chat is usable and mostly complete, but lacks best-in-class polish or proof surfacing.
5: Chat works but has rough states or hidden mechanics.

9: Files, symbols, docs, URLs, git refs, terminal, problems, debugger, and tools inject gracefully with citations.
7: Several context providers are reliable, but breadth or citations are incomplete.
5: Manual file/context attachment only.

9: Per-tool, per-command, per-hunk approval with rollback, policy gates, and clear risk explanations.
7: Approvals are present and useful, but granularity or policy integration is incomplete.
5: Coarse allow/deny flow.

9: Managed preview runs, hot reloads, exposes DOM/screenshots/logs, and drives automated repair.
7: Preview works for common apps but repair loop or isolation is incomplete.
5: Can launch or view a preview manually.

9: Autonomous tasks plan, execute, verify, recover, stop correctly, and produce reviewable proof.
7: Autonomy handles bounded tasks but needs stronger replanning, sandboxing, or proof.
5: Agent loop works for simple tasks with frequent user steering.

9: Editable plan, dependencies, per-step execution, rollback, progress, and proof are visible.
7: Plan/act flow exists but editing or proof is incomplete.
5: Plans are generated but weakly connected to execution.

9: Browser/computer observation and action loops robustly research docs, test UIs, and repair failures.
7: Browser use works for bounded flows but is not deeply integrated.
5: Can call browser tools manually with limited repair use.

9: High-signal PR review comments are severity-ranked, fixable, visible in IDE/PR, and validated by outcomes.
7: PR review works but lacks strong false-positive suppression or IDE polish.
5: Review summaries exist but are not dependable.

9: Watch failures, inline diagnostics, repair prompts, and passing proof are connected across CLI and IDE.
7: Tests run and feed repair loops, but watch/IDE integration is incomplete.
5: Tests are callable but not integrated into the agent loop.

9: Stack frames, watches, locals, logs, failing requests, and traces drive repairs.
7: Debug context exists but is partial or not broadly wired.
5: Basic error output parsing only.

9: Memory is cited, fresh, editable, outcome-proven, and improves later task success.
7: Memory is useful but causal outcome proof or governance is incomplete.
5: Persistence exists without strong relevance control.

9: Skills/plugins are discoverable, versioned, policy-gated, testable, portable, and publishable.
7: Skill/plugin architecture is real but ecosystem DX or governance is incomplete.
5: Plugins exist but are hard to build or trust.

9: Main-path sandboxing, secret scanning, policy enforcement, audit logs, and prompt-injection controls are proven.
7: Strong safety components exist but enforcement is incomplete in some paths.
5: Security checks exist but can be bypassed or are advisory.

9: Checkpoint restore, safe resume, circuit breakers, loop detection, and failure-mode tests are universal.
7: Rollback/recovery exists but does not cover all main workflows.
5: Manual git rollback or partial checkpoints.

9: MCP/tool discovery, schema validation, approvals, logs, docs, and third-party server proof are mature.
7: MCP support is real but ecosystem polish or observability is incomplete.
5: Tool bridge exists with limited validation.

9: Routing adapts by task, quality, privacy, cost, offline state, and provider health with failure tests.
7: Multi-provider routing is useful but adaptation or proof is incomplete.
5: Multiple providers exist but manual selection dominates.

9: Cost per successful task, budgets, caching, fallback savings, and spend UX are proven.
7: Cost controls exist but are not outcome-aware.
5: Token/cost display exists with limited actionability.

9: SSO, RBAC, org model, audit export, admin policy, retention, privacy, and support docs are production-ready.
7: Enterprise foundations exist but lack real IdP/org/admin workflows.
5: Some enterprise-shaped config or audit features exist.
3: Mostly single-user with limited admin controls.

9: Reproducible evals, traces, dashboards, regression gates, and score deltas guide development.
7: Eval harnesses exist but breadth or automation is incomplete.
5: Some fixtures or local evals exist.

9: Users can inspect why the agent acted, evidence used, risks, remaining uncertainty, and undo paths.
7: Proof and rationale are visible but not consistently steerable.
5: Some summaries exist without strong trust mechanics.

9: Context is cited, scoped, expired, deduped, inspectable, user-controllable, and usefulness-measured.
7: Context controls exist but governance or measurement is incomplete.
5: Context is gathered with weak provenance.
3: Prompt stuffing or manual context only.

9: Users can interrupt, steer, share edit state, hand off tasks, pause/resume, and inspect subagents smoothly.
7: Collaboration is usable but lacks mid-run steering or handoff polish.
5: Human approval exists but collaboration is coarse.

9: Vague tasks become dependency-aware, safe, sequenced, verifiable subtasks with completion proof.
7: Decomposition is useful but weakly measured or incomplete for complex tasks.
5: Plans are broken into broad steps only.

9: Release gates stay green, regressions are blocked, and score claims are impossible with failing gates.
7: Regression gates exist but do not yet block every risky claim or path.
5: Tests exist but gate discipline is inconsistent.

9: Fresh users complete a real first task in under 10 minutes with guided recovery and clear setup.
7: Onboarding works but needs polish, telemetry, or beta proof.
5: Setup is documented but easy to derail.

9: CLI, IDE, CI, local/cloud models, MCP, and hosted workflows share a coherent portable runtime.
7: Portability is real but surface parity or packaging is incomplete.
5: Some surfaces work independently.

9: Model, prompt, budget, and retry policy adapt by task, failure, cost, and outcome history.
7: Adaptive routing exists but lacks outcome-driven learning.
5: Static routing with some heuristics.

9: p50/p95 SLOs are met across chat, tools, FIM, indexing, previews, and apply paths.
7: Important paths are measured and mostly responsive.
5: Some latency data exists but UX can block or feel slow.

9: Cost, latency, models, tool calls, failures, evals, and score movement are traceable in dashboards.
7: Logs and artifacts are strong but dashboards or correlation are incomplete.
5: Logs exist but are fragmented.

9: Provider, model, policy, tool, memory, and workspace setup is guided, validated, recoverable, and documented.
7: Configuration is powerful but still has friction or weak validation.
5: Configuration works for experts.

9: Useful behavior survives provider, network, index, credential, and tool failures with clear fallback UX.
7: Offline/local fallback exists but some degraded paths are rough.
5: Limited local fallback.

9: Redaction, retention, telemetry opt-out, local-only mode, and admin-enforced privacy policy are proven.
7: Good privacy primitives exist but lack full admin/product integration.
5: Basic local-first behavior or warnings.
3: Privacy mostly relies on provider behavior.

9: Docs include quickstart, recipes, troubleshooting, API, architecture, migration, and release guidance.
7: Docs are useful but incomplete for public beta users.
5: Internal docs are stronger than public docs.

9: Extension authors get templates, tests, manifests, local dev tools, validation, docs, and publish flow.
7: Extensibility is real but contributor ergonomics need polish.
5: Extension points exist but are underdocumented.

9: Benchmark claims include commands, seeds, traces, raw logs, score history, and limitations.
7: Benchmark results are reproducible internally but not fully public or comparable.
5: Some benchmark-like evidence exists.

9: Huge repos, workspace graphs, dependency boundaries, and cross-repo changes are measured and reliable.
7: Monorepo support exists but lacks large-scale proof.
5: Works on moderate repos.

9: Repair/generation is proven across major languages and frameworks with language-aware tooling.
7: Several languages work but proof is uneven.
5: One stack is strong; others are incidental.

9: Keyboard, screen reader, contrast, focus, motion, readable review UX, and accessibility CI are proven.
7: Major accessibility requirements are handled but not fully measured.
5: Basic accessibility hygiene only.
1: Known inaccessible UX or no consideration.

9: Env vars, Docker, CI, deploy logs, cloud config, services, and runtime failures are understood and repaired.
7: Environment awareness exists but deploy/runtime intelligence is incomplete.
5: Basic dev-server and env handling.

9: Failures and successes safely become evals, lessons, routing policies, prompts, and regression gates.
7: Learning loop exists but lacks full causal proof or governance.
5: Lessons are recorded but weakly operationalized.
